policy_statement: Increase storage of surface water runoff by constructing new dams.
mechanism_description: Constructing new dams increases storage of surface water runoff, improving water supply reliability and resilience to drought and variable rainfall.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/precipitation variability) → ↑ storage/buffering capacity → ↓ exposure/sensitivity of water supply system → ↓ water-scarcity impacts
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Build and operate new dams/reservoirs to capture and store surface runoff and regulate releases. (2) Mediating physical process: Increased stored water and regulated flow smooth inter-annual/seasonal variability, enabling carryover storage from wet periods to dry periods and stabilizing supply. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Greater resilience and reliability of municipal/industrial/agricultural water supply under drought and rainfall variability, reducing service disruptions and associated socioeconomic impacts.
dominant_pathway_test: Expanding surface-water storage most strongly improves water-supply reliability during drought and variable rainfall, reducing exposure to water scarcity impacts.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: The core effect is buffering against climate hazards (drought/variable precipitation) by increasing storage, which reduces exposure and vulnerability—an adaptation pathway. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although dams may enable low-carbon electricity or reduce energy for pumping in some contexts (mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits), these are not inherent to “storage expansion for supply reliability,” and can even be offset by reservoir emissions; the consistent, intended effect is resilience of water supply. 3) Edge cases applied: If a policy is explicitly for hydropower generation or emissions reduction, it may shift categories, but that would be a different canonical mechanism; here the mechanism explicitly targets water-supply resilience. 4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy expanding surface-water storage to improve reliability functions through hazard buffering and therefore fits Adaptation as the primary category. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., drought adaptation, critical infrastructure resilience, co-benefit mitigation) is deferred to policy-level evidence and context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Reservoir expansion can increase GHG emissions (construction and reservoir methane) or provide mitigation via hydropower/avoided pumping; however, those effects are context-specific co-effects and do not change the dominant adaptation pathway when the stated purpose is water-supply resilience.
key_indicators: “Increasing the storage of surface water runoff”; “constructing new dams”; “Storage of Surface Water Runoff”
co_benefits: Hydropower generation potential; flood control/peak-flow attenuation; irrigation/municipal water supply reliability; recreation and local economic activity
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also serve non-climate objectives (e.g., hydropower, general water-supply development), but the stated mechanism is explicitly increasing runoff storage, which is directly aligned with drought/variability adaptation.
